disease.name: Lung Abscess
disease.note: A lung abscess is a necrotic cavity containing pus, most often complicating aspiration pneumonia. Putrid sputum is characteristic. Prolonged IV antibiotics are mainstay; bronchoscopic drainage or surgical resection for refractory cases.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Higher risk of rupture and bronchopleural fistula

qualitativeModifiers.description: Foul-smelling sputum is characteristic of anaerobic lung abscess

historyFeatures.note: May be blood-streaked; massive haemoptysis is a danger sign
Loss of consciousness, dysphagia, seizures, alcoholism, sedation

examFeatures.note: Develops after 4–8 weeks of chronic infection

riskFactors.note: Alcoholism, seizure disorder, cerebrovascular accident, drug overdose, dementia, general anaesthesia

subtypes.name: Aspiration Lung Abscess (Anaerobe)
subtypes.management: Prolonged IV antibiotics: clindamycin 600–900 mg IV q8h or penicillin G 2–4 MU IV q4–6h; step-down to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin for 4–8 weeks total

subtypes.name: Secondary Lung Abscess (Bacterial/Aerobic)
subtypes.management: IV antibiotics based on culture; consider MRSA coverage (vancomycin, linezolid); pseudomonas coverage if risk factors

subtypes.name: Tuberculous Lung Abscess
subtypes.management: Standard antitubercular therapy (RHZE); rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol for 2 months, then 4 months of RH

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry
bedside.indication: all febrile patients with respiratory symptoms

laboratory.name: FBC + CRP + procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: leucocytosis, left shift, elevated inflammatory markers

laboratory.name: Sputum Gram stain and culture (aerobic + anaerobic)
laboratory.indication: identify pathogen; anaerobic culture requires proper transport

imaging.name: CT chest with IV contrast
imaging.indication: define abscess size, wall thickness, air-fluid level, rule out underlying mass
imaging.typicalFindings: Cavitary lesion with thick irregular wall, air-fluid level, surrounding parenchymal consolidation, associated empyema possible

advanced.name: Bronchoscopy with protected brush/BAL
advanced.indication: when sputum non-diagnostic, suspected obstructing lesion, or failure to improve
advanced.note: Can obtain diagnostic specimens and drain abscess if endobronchial

advanced.name: CT-guided percutaneous aspiration
advanced.indication: large abscess not accessible bronchoscopically, or suspicion of unusual pathogen

management.protocolReference: BTS Guidelines on Lung Abscess (2024); ATS/ERS Community-Acquired Pneumonia Guidelines

pathways.criteria: small abscess <4 cm, no danger signs, good oral intake, immunocompetent
treatment: IV antibiotics: clindamycin 600–900 mg IV q8h OR penicillin G 2–4 million units IV q4–6h
Alternative IV regimens: ampicillin-sulbactam 1.5–3 g q6h, amoxicillin-clavulanate, piperacillin-tazobactam
Switch to oral when patient is afebrile and improving: amoxicillin-clavulanate 625 mg PO q8h or clindamycin 300 mg PO q6h
Total duration: 4–8 weeks (guided by clinical and radiological resolution)
Chest physiotherapy and postural drainage for dependent segments
pathways.followUp: CT chest at 4 weeks to confirm resolution

pathways.criteria: abscess >6 cm, failure to improve after 2 weeks of IV antibiotics, immunocompromised, large air-fluid level
treatment: Extended IV antibiotics for complete course (may require 8–12 weeks)
Consider bronchoscopic drainage: aspiration of pus, placement of pigtail catheter
CT-guided percutaneous catheter drainage if bronchoscopic approach fails or inaccessible
- Abscess persists >6–8 weeks despite medical therapy
Empiric MRSA coverage (vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV q12h, linezolid 600 mg IV/PO q12h) if risk factors
Empiric pseudomonas coverage (cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, levofloxacin) if immunocompromised or structural lung disease
pathways.escalation: Multidisciplinary input: respiratory, interventional pulmonology, thoracic surgery, infectious diseases

disease.emergencyPriority: if massive haemoptysis, sepsis shock, or acute respiratory failure
diagnosticClues: cavitary lesion with air-fluid level on CT chest
clinical aspiration history (loss of consciousness, dysphagia, seizures)
periodontal disease and poor oral hygiene
response to prolonged IV clindamycin or penicillin G
exclusionClues: upper lobe cavity without air-fluid level and positive GeneXpert suggests TB
thin-walled cavity suggests pneumatocoele or bulla
community-acquired pneumonia without cavitation is not a lung abscess
